speaker: interviewer
text: There you go. Are you plugged back in again? Are you plugged?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's plugged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, sounds good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, today is November 5th, 1995. We're at the home of Fred Hofmann, conducting an interview for the Shoah Foundation, here in Denver, Colorado. OK, Fred, what took place for you after your father died? And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] I-- we were still in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The factory that we were working at, that Braubach gasoline factory, it was bombarded twice during that period we were there, in that Rehmsdorf camp. We happen to-- with the German instructors-- to rebuild it, keep it going again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One time, they made a mistake and they throw a bomb in the camp. And quite a few people got killed on the-- on that-- from that bomb. It was always at nighttime. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After they bombarded and destroyed a-- the factory, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those SS were very angry and mad. And they were kickin' us and beatin' us, and puttin' all the revenge out on us. And they kept on tellin' us, we will kill you all out. No one of you will ever survive. That, we had, every day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --just lost. [SIGHS] I didn't know what to do. We didn't have anybody to talk to. Everybody was wild. Nobody was trying to-- to be friendly. Everybody was trying just to take care about themselves. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It took [SIGHS] quite a while, two times, to rebuild the factory and make it gone. It was starting to get cold, gettin' toward the fall. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They would not let us in the barracks. They kept us out-- outside on the-- on the-- on the yard. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was just terrible. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And that was going on for quite a while. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the winter time, when it was zero-below weather, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to put a little paper around our body, to keep our body warm a little bit. Usually, they used to catch us. And they used to search us to see we did it. And we got beaten up very much, with their rifles. And they were kicking us with their boots. One cold day in the factory, [SIGHS] they had a little fire there. And I was very cold. And I went over to that fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I warmed up my hands a little bit. And I got caught by SS man. He says to me, what I'm doing there? He asked me from what commando I am. So I told him, I'm from the [INAUDIBLE] [? commando, ?] And I just came to warm up my hands a little bit. He says, you're not supposed to do that. So he went, and he took like, a broomstick, and started beatin' me up so badly that I passed out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He left me there. I could feel quite a few kicks from his boots. And I don't know how long he took. And I got alive again. And I ran back to the commando. And I would never do it again. I couldn't. I was afraid to do it. So I was very badly beaten that one time. And the life was just going on the same way, day after day. Walk, march to the factory, come back to the camp. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And living on the little food we were eating, we were getting weaker all the time. No clothes, just the same thin, light clothes. We were very dirty. We had a lot of lice on our body. The buys-- the lice were crawling all over us, sucking our blood, what we had left in our body. We never took showers. They gave us little soaps to wash our hands sometime. On the soaps were three initials, J-R-F. And we found out that those soaps are made of the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Rein Juden Fett, right-- of the fat of the bodies, what they were cremating. We didn't want to use those soaps. We just throw it away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were-- maybe in the whole time were were in the camp, we had-- maybe they took us out to take one or two showers in the whole time. No change in clothes. And we were just eaten up by the lice. They were crawlin' all over us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We survive somehow. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1945, around April, whatever was left in that camp, they put us together. They put us on trains and were takin' us who knows where? We didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we went on those trains, in those wagons. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Americans bombarded the railroad, quite far ahead of the train. We could see it. The train slowed down. They told us to jump out of the train, of the wagons, and run in, in a little forest there. Some people got killed jumping out from there. Some people could run. They surrounded. And we were in that forest. The train kept on going slowly. They surrounded that forest. And they gathered us together again. Next to me was a dead bird. I grabbed that bird and I ate it up. I was glad I had something to eat on. They gathered us together. And they divided us in groups of about 50. And from there, we had to march. We found out that we are coming into Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We marched for about a week, day and night. No, at night time, we didn't, but during the day. At night time, they used to put us in those-- by those farms, in those barns, to sleep over. And next morning, we had to get up and march again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't feed us. We were grabbing grass from the field. Because as soon we have some place coming up, they told us to lay down. And we were just putting grass from the field, from the side of the street. And we were eatin' grass. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They didn't give us any food at all. That was-- the hunger was unbearable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- again, some people had blankets they carried with them. They couldn't-- they didn't have power to carry. So they just throw it away on the side. Some people couldn't march anymore. We could not help 'em out, to help 'em. They were just left on behind, behind, behind. I don't know, from about 3,000 people in the camp, if-- if 250 to 300 all over-- 300 people were alive on the end. We arrived to [SIGHS] Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was a little better, in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us a little better food. That camp, they kept for a sample. It's going to fall down. That camp was kept for a sample, in case somebody wants to come and check, to see, to show the world that they didn't treat us so bad. But in that Theresienstadt, they were building a crematorium. Like they had in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they tried to destroy us and kill us all out there. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Apparently, they didn't succeed. They didn't have enough time. We could hear a lot of sounds from-- from guns, from machine guns, from-- from tanks at night time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thought maybe it's-- or the war is getting stronger, or something is going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was so sick when I was there. I couldn't walk on my feet anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were there for about one week in Theresienstadt. And it was a little better than the other camps were there. They give us a little bit more food to eat. We could see women with children in that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we don't know what-- what is going on. We'd had no idea. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On May 10th, 1945, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we wake up in the morning. And we see Russian soldiers around us. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] They were very nice to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] They told us we shouldn't worry. The war is over. And we will survive. We will be OK. Apparently, the Germans, that night, throw away the rifles. They gave themselves up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were happy, that thank God, we are out of this Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They start feeding us, [SIGHS] helping us, putting us in certain places, giving us a lot of food. Throwing boxes with cans, with meats, and soups and fat stuff and bread, and all kinds of things. Packages after packages. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] We started grabbing that food and eatin'. Food, finally. We see some food in front of us. Plenty. [CRYING] I was grabbing bread. My main desire was to eat bread. I didn't care about meats. I didn't care about cans. I was grabbing the bread. And I was eating bread, day after day. Once in a while, I used to get a little piece of meat and eat with the bread. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In a few days, we could see people laying on the floor, sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had diarrhea. Their stomach was not used to those fats-- fat food. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And they were dying. They couldn't have medication so fast. And there was a lot of people just laying all over the floors, all over. They were just plain dead people, from all this stuff they start eating. [SIGHS] They were very nice to us, the soldiers, the Russian army. And they kept on telling us that we will be OK. We shouldn't worry. The war is over. We didn't see a German in front of us anymore. No SS, no nobody. We started walking around a little bit. Some Russian soldiers captured some Germans. And they used to call us, come here. Beat them up. Now you have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who could beat? We could hardly pick up our arms. We couldn't do nothing. So they were taking their rifles and they were beating them. They were hitting them. They were taking revenge. And they say, this is the way to do it, to beat them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we lost a lot of people, just like you, that they killed a lot of our guys out. But they were running so fast, away, that they tried to be captured by the Americans instead of the Russians. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember at that time, wanting to have revenge?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, and I start kicking them a little bit. But I couldn't do nothing. I-- I could hardly walk on my feet myself. So what was the use? It was no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? When ?] in Theresienstadt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met some friends there. They were also like me. We were walking to get some bread, some soup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We got start feeling a little better, a little stronger. But our mind was gone. We thought we don't know if we are alive or we are dead. It was just-- just impossible to-- to do anything with our mind, to remember. But we know that we are happy that there is no more Germans around us. When we got a little bit well, when we got a little bit better, they told us we can go home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They will take us to Budapest. And there is a-- a big building. And all the people from that area, what they took us away, they will meet over there. They will find out who is alive, who isn't alive, who lives, who isn't, from the villages, from the cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us to Hungary, to Budapest. They put us there, in a big school, a building or something. And we were taken care of by-- by the Joint. It's like a Jewish Family Service. They fed us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and we were looking, every day, the new transport were coming in, what names they put out there, who came. While I was there, some guy from my town told me, your brother is alive. The one what was in the Hungarian army. Not the one what-- what-- what got killed, but the other one. And he will come here, pretty soon, in a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was really happy. That was a big happiness for me, that my brother is-- is alive. I don't know nothing about my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Took about three days, my brother arrived in the same place. He is my older brother. He says he went home. Nothing is left. Everything is destroyed. And let's wait. Maybe somebody will show up yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we waited there. We lived there. We were well taken care of.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I walked down the street by that building. And I see a girl walking toward me. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: We come closer and closer. And we recognize each other. She says, Fraim, are you this? Is that you, Fraim, Freddy? I said, yes, yes, I [? am. ?] [INAUDIBLE]. So that was another happiness. We-- we start crying. So the three of us were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She survived the camp too. So we were there for a few weeks. And we decide to think what to do. To go home, there's no use to go. They told us that we can go to some other countries. We can do anything we want to. We can travel anywhere we want to. But it will take a while. [SIGHS] We knew that nobody else is going to be coming anymore because I told them my father, my mother, what happened to them. They told us if you go back to Germany, you will be taken care of by the American, Joint, Jewish Family Service. You will live in the display person camps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You will start doing a-- thinking of a future for yourself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wanted, very badly, to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We came back to Germany. We wind up being in a D-- DP camp called Leipheim. There, people were workin' in the camp, learnin' trades. They had that ORT school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We wanted to go to Israel very badly. We said, we had enough of this [INAUDIBLE], as they say, being in those countries where they hate us, and we are always being hated. But we didn't have a-- Israel was not Israel. It was hard to get in there. They didn't let nobody in. So we were hoping someday we will get in. We met some-- I joined a-- a-- a-- a youth group that they had some Israeli teachers and leaders, and telling us stories about Israel. And they told us that slowly, we will wind up being in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother says he is going to go first and see if he can get in. So he went to Italy, from Germany. He met a girl there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He got married. She had relatives in New York. He was trying to go to Israel. I was still in the same DP camp. He wrote us, it's impossible to get into Israel. They can't. And his wife's family is going to bring her to America. So we should hold off and see what's going to happen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Meantime, I got sick in the camp. I broke down with double pneumonia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The pneumonia led to tuberculosis. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I really got very sick. And I thought, I'm going to lose my life again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put me in the hospital. They start treating me. I start feeling a little bit better. It took a whole year to cure the tuberculosis. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Thank God, I got cured. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a year, when the doctor said that I'm pretty-- pretty well, healthy, but I still need a lot of rest. They were afraid that it might return or come back. I should go to a sanatorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they send me to a sanatorium over in [INAUDIBLE]. And there, we were just resting and trying to get real strong. And thank God, the TB never returned to me. It got cured. Up till today, I'm well. I'm healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned a trade, tailoring. They had those ORT schools. Because they told us, if you want to go to America, it will be easier if you have a certificate like this. So I learned that tailoring there. And I registered. My brother said I should come to America. If there will be some day easier to get into Israel, we can always go later on. And we suffered so long. And in Israel, at that time, was fighting around too much. It was no peace there. He says, we should come to America. So my sister got married in the camp. And she moved out to Canada. And I was the last one left there. I registered. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in 1951, I got the visa. I came out to New York. Took about 10 days on the ship. We came on the ship, what they were taking army to Germany and bringing them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came out of the ship. I came with [? Emil ?] [? Hecht. ?] I don't know, he's a friend of mine. You know him. We were on the same boat. We arrive to New York. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take me down to Ellis Island. Because they knew I had tuberculosis. They check me up in Eilis Island. And they let me out. They wouldn't send me back. They said, you are OK. You can enter the country. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date you arrived, Fred?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was around-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --January 15, '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your-- your-- your feelings when you got off the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so happy, that there was nothing in my world better than to meet my brother and sister alive, and-- and to arrive to this country. But I was also worried a little. What would I do here? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They knew that I have a brother in-- living in Brooklyn. The Jewish Family Service called up my brother. And they said, go to Ellis Island and pick up your brother, and take care of him. My brother came. Took me from Ellis Island to his house. And I lived with him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I was feeling good already. I was healthy. I wanted to do something for myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went down to the Joint, to the Jewish Family Service. And I told them that I came, and I'm looking for a job. I want to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they say, do you speak English? I say, I don't. But I speak Yiddish. So they put me in-- they found me a job at the Garment District there, in those sweatshops, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as an operator, working on clothes. They found me a guy, that he was Italian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he spoke Yiddish like I did. So I could understand him. I went there. I worked on piece work. I made a few dollars for myself, whatever I did. But the language, I didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to night school. I said, I'm going to learn English. I have to. So I went to night school. I learned English. I worked in daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was making a few good dollars. I said to my brother, you know, I think I can get myself an apartment and just try to make for myself, start going on my own. He says, you can do it. But if you have any problems, you know, I'm your brother. I'll take care of you. I'll-- I'll help you in any way. Thank God, I found an apartment. I moved out. I lived there for two years. For some reason, New York was too big for me, too big of a city. I wasn't-- I was happy in one way. And-- and I kind of-- of scared. Even up till today, I am afraid of a big crowd. Since the camp, because I was lost all alone. I was always-- between big crowds. And-- and that put some fear in me. And New York is very crowded. So I said-- I had some friends here in Colorado. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was thinking about coming and visit them. I saved up a few dollars. I took the train. I came out to visit them. In 1950-- '53, I came out here. I was working, two years, in the factories in New York.
